title: Dog White Ulcer Spot on Eyeball: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is a white spot on your dog's eye an emergency? Learn about corneal ulcers, urgency levels, and how to help your vet with remote triage.
content_html:
What is a White Spot or Ulcer on a Dog's Eye?

A white spot on your dog’s eyeball is often a sign of a corneal ulcer, which is an open sore on the clear front surface of the eye. This cloudiness or white patch can also be caused by corneal scarring, mineral deposits, or an underlying infection. These conditions typically occur due to physical trauma, chemical irritants, or underlying health issues like dry eye (KCS).

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for a white spot on the eye is rated as Medium. While it may not require an immediate trip to an ER in the middle of the night, eye issues can deteriorate very quickly. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 hours. If your dog is showing signs of extreme pain, such as constant squinting, pawing at the eye, or if the eye looks sunken, seek care as soon as possible to prevent permanent vision loss.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a high-quality, well-lit photo of your dog's eye can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. A clear image allows a professional to evaluate the size, depth, and exact location of the spot. This information helps them determine if the case is a routine appointment or if you need to be fast-tracked into the clinic to save the eye's integrity.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my dog's white spot?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human eye medications on a dog without professional advice, as some ingredients can worsen certain types of ulcers or cause severe irritation.

faqs.question: What are common signs of eye pain in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include squinting, excessive blinking, watery discharge, and pawing or rubbing the face against furniture or the floor.

faqs.question: How do vets diagnose a corneal ulcer?
faqs.answer: Veterinarians typically use a fluorescein stain test, where a non-toxic green dye is placed in the eye to highlight any breaks or sores on the corneal surface.
